question_text: Which statement best describes the RN case manager's role in utilization review?
answer_a: Provide current clinical facts, skilled need indicators, patient risks, and follow-up needs for review
answer_b: Decide whether the payer must cover the service
answer_c: Change diagnoses so services are more likely to be authorized
answer_d: Avoid patient-care details because utilization review is only administrative
rationale: The RN case manager supports utilization review by communicating accurate clinical facts and patient-care context while the payer retains authority for coverage decisions.

question_text: A payer asks for additional information before reviewing continued skilled nursing visits. Which response is most appropriate?
answer_a: Tell the patient the visits will be covered once the payer sees the request
answer_b: Submit current wound status, skilled interventions, functional limits, caregiver barriers, and follow-up request through the approved process
answer_c: Send only the diagnosis name and ask for all future visits to be approved
answer_d: Wait until the review period closes before documenting the request
rationale: A complete information response links the requested service to current assessment findings, skilled interventions, barriers, and next steps without promising approval.

question_text: Which phrase is the most payer-safe?
answer_a: The payer has to approve this because the patient needs it
answer_b: This denial is wrong and will be reversed
answer_c: RN submitted current clinical summary for payer review; coverage decision remains pending
answer_d: Patient was promised that insurance will pay for continued visits
rationale: The correct wording documents the action taken and pending status while avoiding guarantees, blame, or conclusions outside the RN role.

question_text: During prior authorization support, which task should be escalated rather than handled independently by the RN case manager?
answer_b: Documenting when clinical information was submitted
answer_c: Explaining to the patient that payer review is pending
answer_d: Creating provider rationale for an order that is not documented
rationale: Provider rationale and order clarification belong with the provider or designated clinical authority; the RN should coordinate the request instead of inventing justification.

question_text: A continued-visit authorization is delayed for a patient with an active wound and limited caregiver availability. What should the RN case manager prioritize?
answer_a: Document patient-care risk, follow the approved review process, and escalate if delay threatens safe continuity
answer_b: Tell the family the payer will be responsible for any bad outcome
answer_c: Stop documenting until the authorization decision is final
answer_d: Promise the patient that the agency will provide all visits regardless of payer decision
rationale: Authorization delays require a safety-focused documentation trail and timely escalation when patient risk may increase, while avoiding threats or coverage promises.

question_text: A payer denies continued visits because the record does not clearly show ongoing skilled need. Which RN action is safest?
answer_a: Rewrite prior documentation so the skilled need sounds stronger
answer_b: Document the denial reason if available, notify appropriate roles, identify patient risks, and coordinate appeal-support facts within policy
answer_c: Tell the patient that all denials are administrative errors
answer_d: Ignore the denial because appeal work is never related to patient care
rationale: The safest response preserves record integrity, documents the stated reason, communicates patient-care risk, and routes appeal support through the correct process.

question_text: A patient asks whether the requested service will definitely be covered. Which response best fits M07 guidance?
answer_a: Yes, because the RN believes the service is needed
answer_b: No, because payer decisions cannot be discussed with patients
answer_c: Coverage decisions are made by the payer; I will submit allowed clinical information and document follow-up
answer_d: The provider can guarantee coverage if the RN sends enough notes
rationale: The RN can explain the process and continue advocacy, but should not guarantee payer decisions or shut down appropriate patient education.

question_text: A payer requests diagnosis clarification not found in the nursing record. What should the RN case manager do?
answer_a: Guess the most likely diagnosis based on symptoms
answer_b: Send a broad statement that all services are medically necessary
answer_c: Tell the patient to call the payer and resolve it alone
answer_d: Coordinate provider clarification through the approved process and document the request
rationale: When diagnosis or order clarification is missing, the RN coordinates with the provider or proper role and documents the follow-up rather than speculating.

question_text: Which rewrite best improves the risky phrase, The payer must approve more visits?
answer_a: Current wound findings, teaching response, mobility limits, and caregiver availability were submitted for payer review of continued skilled nursing visits
answer_b: The payer is expected to approve because the patient has a diagnosis
answer_c: Approval should be automatic because the family is worried
answer_d: The RN guaranteed continued services after speaking with the patient
rationale: The rewrite uses documented clinical facts and a neutral review request instead of a demand or promise of authorization.

question_text: Which documentation element is most important after a payer contact?
answer_a: The RN's personal opinion about whether the payer is cooperative
answer_b: Reason for contact, information sent, recipient or channel, date/time, pending decision, and next follow-up
answer_c: Only the patient's diagnosis because the rest can be remembered later
answer_d: A statement that the outcome is certain if the chart is complete
rationale: A payer contact note should allow another reviewer to reconstruct the request, what was shared, where it went, what is pending, and what follow-up is due.

question_text: Which appeal-support activity is within RN case-management scope when allowed by policy?
answer_a: Writing a legal argument for the patient
answer_b: Changing the provider order to match payer criteria
answer_c: Contributing current assessment findings, patient response, skilled care history, and risk information
answer_d: Promising the denial will be overturned if the RN participates
rationale: The RN may provide clinical facts and coordination history for appeal support, but should not provide legal claims, alter orders, or promise appeal outcomes.

question_text: Which situation most clearly requires escalation beyond routine RN payer communication?
answer_a: The payer portal confirms receipt of a routine update
answer_b: The patient asks what date the request was submitted
answer_c: The RN documents that review remains pending
answer_d: The payer request appears inconsistent with permitted disclosure or agency policy
rationale: Unusual disclosure or policy concerns should be escalated to supervisor or compliance channels because they may involve privacy, contractual, or regulatory risk.

question_text: Which action best demonstrates patient advocacy during a utilization review delay?
answer_a: Explain the pending process, provide safety instructions, notify the provider if risk increases, and track follow-up
answer_b: Tell the patient that advocacy means promising approval
answer_c: Avoid discussing symptoms until the payer decides
answer_d: Document only the payer phone number and no patient-care risk
rationale: Advocacy remains active through clear education, safety follow-up, provider notification when needed, and tracking unresolved barriers without guaranteeing coverage.

question_text: What must remain true about certificate release for this Draft / Pending BRN CEP Approval course content?
answer_a: The M07 quiz pass alone releases a certificate
answer_b: Certificate release is blocked until provider approval metadata and required completion evidence gates are satisfied
answer_c: A certificate can be released when the learner views all pages
answer_d: The module may advertise approval after the assignment is graded
rationale: Passing one module quiz is not enough; certificate release requires provider approval metadata and full completion evidence according to the course gate rules.

question_text: Which statement best separates clinical facts from promises?
answer_a: The payer will approve because the wound still looks bad
answer_b: The patient will be unsafe unless every requested visit is covered
answer_c: RN observed drainage, documented teaching needs, submitted allowed clinical facts, and noted payer decision pending
answer_d: The family wants services, so payment is guaranteed
rationale: This statement identifies observed facts, nursing action, submitted information, and pending status without predicting authorization, payment, or outcome.
